title: Thai Larb-Style Minced Chicken Salad

ingredients.name: minced chicken
visual_checks: murgh keema; ask for coarsely ground, not paste-fine
ingredients.notes: murgh keema; ask for coarsely ground, not paste-fine

subs.name: minced pork or soya chunks (soaked and squeezed) for a vegetarian version

ingredients.name: raw rice, toasted and ground to powder
visual_checks: dry-toast in a pan until golden, then grind coarsely in mixie
ingredients.notes: dry-toast in a pan until golden, then grind coarsely in mixie

ingredients.name: shallots or small red onions, thinly sliced

subs.name: 4 tbsp tamarind water for a more tropical sourness
2 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt

ingredients.name: dried red chilli flakes
visual_checks: adjust to taste; larb is traditionally very hot
ingredients.notes: adjust to taste; larb is traditionally very hot

subs.name: extra coriander or Vietnamese coriander (rau ram), if available

steps.text: Toast the raw rice in a dry pan over medium heat, shaking frequently, for 3-4 minutes until the grains are evenly golden and smell nutty. Let cool for 2 minutes, then grind coarsely in a mixie or mortar — it should have the texture of coarse sand, not fine powder. This toasted rice powder (khao khua) is what gives larb its distinctive textured bite.

Heat oil in a pan on medium-high heat. Add the minced chicken and cook, breaking it up constantly, for 6-8 minutes until fully cooked through and slightly caramelised. There should be no pink visible. Remove from heat and let it cool for 5 minutes.

In a large mixing bowl, combine the warm chicken mince with lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, chilli flakes and toasted rice powder. Toss well — the lime juice and fish sauce are the dressing, not a garnish, so mix thoroughly.

Add the sliced shallots, spring onion, mint leaves and coriander. Toss gently to combine everything without bruising the herbs. Taste: the salad should be lip-smackingly sour, salty, spicy and just barely sweet. Add more lime for sourness, fish sauce for saltiness or chilli for heat.

Pile onto a serving plate. Serve immediately with raw cucumber slices, cabbage wedges or a few balls of sticky rice on the side for wrapping. The herbs will wilt if left too long, so this dish must be eaten soon after tossing.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → minced pork or soya chunks (soaked and squeezed) for a vegetarian version; fish-sauce → 2 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt; lemon → 4 tbsp tamarind water for a more tropical sourness; mint → extra coriander or Vietnamese coriander (rau ram), if available
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Minced chicken (murgh keema) is available at every Indian chicken shop — ask for a coarse grind. A mixie or small food processor grinds the toasted rice to the right consistency in 10 seconds. All herbs — mint (pudina) and coriander (kothamalli) — are Indian pantry essentials. This salad requires no special equipment and comes together entirely in a kadai and a mixing bowl.
